title: 2  Current Data Limitations
text:
2.1 Individual-Annual EDM Data Merge Results
The tables below show the results of the merge between: (i) the individual spill data (provided by the water companies) containing the start time and end time of each spill event, and (ii) the annual EDM data (published by the Environment Agency) containing additional useful information, such as the grid coordinates of the discharge points.
The merge between the individual and annual data is important for two reasons:
Individual spill data prior to 2023 lacks unique identifiers for spill sites. Consequently, matching records between the datasets must rely on other variables, such as site names, which can lead to varying degrees of merge success.

title: 2  Current Data Limitations
text:
Enriching Spill Records: The individual data mainly captures event timings. Merging incorporates valuable details from the annual data, such as site grid coordinates, into these individual records.
Assessing Data Quality: The EA’s Annual EDM data undergoes an audit to correct errors. By aggregating individual spills annually and comparing these totals against the audited annual data, we can evaluate the accuracy of the raw individual spill reports.



Perfect match: An individual spill record is matched to a single, unique discharge point in the annual data (a 1-to-1 match based on identifying information like site name).
Partial match: An individual spill record is matched to multiple discharge points in the annual data (a 1-to-many match). Aggregated statistics for these spills therefore relate to multiple discharge points. See Section 2.1.2.1 for details.
No match: An individual spill record could not be matched to any discharge point in the annual data. See Section 2.1.2.2 for details.


OverallCompanyYearCompany-Year




































2.1.1 Background
The Event Duration Monitoring (EDM) data shows sewage discharge events from storm overflows across England’s 10 Water and Sewerage Companies (WaSCs) over three years. The data is comprised of:

Individual spill data: 30 separate datasets (one per company-year combination), containing logs of each discharge event, including start and end times. These records also contain various site identifiers, though their availability and format vary by company and year.
Annual spill data: Contain aggregated annual data on frequency and duration of sewage spills. These data contain additional environmental information not present in the individual records, particularly the grid coordinates of discharge points.

We therefore want to merge discharge points grid location data from the annual data to individual spill data for subsequent geographic economic analysis.


2.1.2 Problems

2.1.2.1 1. Location Data Integration
Problem: The individual data lack consistent unique identifiers to match with annual data.1 This means that we sometimes cannot match a location in the individual spill data with a unique discharge point grid coordinate (3.1% of total observations).
Current Solution: When multiple discharge points in the annual data match a single site in the individual data, we assign the location of the discharge point with the highest annual spill duration. This seems reasonable if these discharge points are close to each other (yet to check but seems plausible).


2.1.2.2 2. Unmatched Observations
Problem: A portion of observations in the individual data cannot be matched with the annual data (1.7% of total observations) despite the use of fuzzy matching.
Potential Solution: Exploring these unmatched observations to check if the many-to-many matching can capture additional observations, or if fuzzy matching can be improved.
For example:

Mismatch of site name

title: 2  Current Data Limitations
text:
2.2 Incorrect Spill Data

2.2.1 Background
We aggregate individual sewage overflow spills into total spill counts and durations based on data affecting the same catchment area. To validate our aggregation, we can compare our results with the annual aggregated data published by the Environment Agency (EA). The comparison yields a correlations over 90% for both spill counts and durations between our manual aggregation and the EA’s figures. The data that seems most problematic are Wessex Water’s 2022 records and United Utilities’ data for all years.

Spill CountSpill Duration





















2.2.2 Problems

2.2.2.1 Anomalous/Incorrect Spills
Problem: The manually aggregated spill counts and spill durations are sometimes much larger than the corresponding EA figures despite the date-time parsing being correct. For example, there are some individual spill events lasting multiple days within the raw data (for which the date-time parsing is correct). These are likely outliers that the EA excludes from their official figures, possibly due to sensor malfunctions.
Potential Solution: Since the criteria for excluding individual spills are unknown, we could experiment with different exclusion thresholds to determine which ones align most closely with the EA’s data.

title: 2  Current Data Limitations
text:
2.3 Archive Problems

2.3.1 Incorrect Spill Data

2.3.1.1 Negative Individual Spill Duration
Problem: There are 7 individual spills for which duration is negative. Many times, this issue occurs when the spill end time is at exactly midnight (00:00:00). Based on the examples below, these negative durations stem from a date parsing error where midnight timestamps are incorrectly assigned to the current day rather than the following day. For instance, a spill ending at “midnight on January 1st” should be interpreted as 00:00:00 on January 2nd.






Solution: I corrected the observations with a negative spill duration for which the end date was exactly midnight (00:00:00) by advancing the end date by one day. I removed the observations with a negative spill duration where it wasn’t clear why the end date was before the start date.


2.3.1.2 2022 Wessex Water Data Does Not Match EA Data
Problem: For the 2022 Wessex Water data, none of the spill counts or durations in the manually aggregated data matches with the EA’s figures.
Solution: I addressed the observations with a negative spill duration as follows:

For cases where the end date was exactly midnight (00:00:00), I advanced the end date by one day.

For cases where the reason for the end date being earlier than the start date was unclear, I removed the observations.

title: 3  Sewage Spill Data Exploration
text:
3.1 What Is a Combined Sewer Overflow and How Does It Work?
A Combined Sewer Overflow (CSO) is a relief point built into older sewerage systems, a legacy of Victorian engineering, where a single pipe carries both household sewage and rainwater runoff. During periods of heavy rainfall, the volume of this mixed wastewater can overwhelm the network’s capacity. To prevent sewage from backing up into streets and homes, the CSO acts as an emergency safety valve. Once the flow reaches a specific level, it overtops an internal weir and is discharged, untreated, directly into a nearby river, lake, or the sea. While designed for use only in exceptional storm conditions, the system’s function is to prioritise the protection of property from flooding over preventing waterway pollution.

3.3.1 Total Spills
The maps below display the total number and duration of sewage overflow spills, aggregated at the Middle Layer Super Output Area (MSOA)—a standard statistical geography in England designed to represent areas with roughly similar population sizes (~8000 people or ~3000 households).
Because MSOAs differ considerably in land area, it’s difficult to draw conclusions. Even so, sewage spills appear relatively evenly distributed across England. Fewer spill sites are visible around London, though this likely reflects the very small geographic size of MSOAs in high-density urban areas rather than a true absence of spills. There also appears to be heavier spill activitiy in the North and South West.

section: 3.4 Frequency and Duration of Sewage Spills
text:
3.4 Frequency and Duration of Sewage Spills
Both the summary tables and the histograms show that the frequency and duration are very positively skewed. A priori, this could be due various factors (also considering that left side is bound at zero):

Malfunctioning of EDM sensors: Malfunctioning EDM sensors may detect ‘blank’ sewage overflows, creating outliers in the right tail.
Extreme weather events: Sewage overflows are (theoretically) caused by rainfall. Episodic heavy rain could create temporal clusters of long-duration events, stretching the right tail.
Aging or Inconsistent Infrastructure: Older sewer systems or uneven maintenance across regions could lead to chronic sewage spills, stretching the right tail

title: 4  Dry Spill Exploration
text: 4.1 What Is a Dry Spill?

title: 4  Dry Spill Exploration
text:
4.1.1 Context
The earliest sewer systems in England, built during the Victorian era, were combined systems, using a single pipe to carry both wastewater from homes and businesses and stormwater from rainfall. Since the 1960s, however, new sewer networks have been constructed as separate systems, with one pipe for sewage and another for surface water in built-up areas. Today, around 20% of England’s sewer network remains combined.
In most cases, wastewater is transported through the sewer network to treatment works, where it is processed and then released into rivers or the sea. However, in certain circumstances, such as periods of heavy rainfall, water companies are permitted to discharge untreated sewage into inland waters through storm overflows to prevent the system from being overwhelmed.

The deliberate discharge of sewage in dry conditions, known as a dry spill, is prohibited under environmental law. Because no rainfall is available to dilute the sewage, dry spills pose a particularly high risk of environmental harm to receiving waters and ecosystems.


4.1.2 Definitions

4.1.2.1 Environmental Agency
The Environment Agency (EA) defines a dry spill as the use of a storm overflow on a dry day, meaning no rainfall above 0.25 mm on that day or during the previous 24 hours.1
Storm overflows are not expected to operate on dry days, although exceptions can occur. In large catchments, for example, rainfall upstream may take significant time to travel downstream before reaching the overflow. For this reason, the EA treats any recorded dry-day spill as a potential breach until further investigation confirms the cause.


4.1.2.2 BBC
In its reporting, the BBC defines a dry day more conservatively: no measurable rainfall (≥0.25 mm) during the preceding four days within a 9 \(km^2\) area around each overflow site.2 This approach was intended to ensure that spills flagged as occurring on dry days could not plausibly be attributed to recent rainfall still moving through the catchment. The 9 \(km^2\) buffer was used as a proxy for the catchment feeding each overflow.



BBC Methodology

title: 4  Dry Spill Exploration
text:
4.2 Estimating Dry Spills
We estimate dry spills using alternative methodologies and validate these against the figures reported by the EA3 and the BBC4.
We present two versions of the dry-spill totals to reflect differences in published samples:

EA sample: aggregates across all companies and sites.
BBC sample: excludes Welsh Water entirely and discounts “more than one-third of United Utilities’ overflows” where “the discharge data provided to the EA did not correspond to their 2022 annual report summarising this data.”

The EA reports 20,000 dry spills in 2022 and 18,000 in 2023. Our closest estimates use the criterion of no measurable rainfall (≥0.25 mm) in the preceding two days within either a \(km^2\) (column 1) or a 9 \(km^2\) buffer (column 2). We prefer the latter, as it is slightly more conservative.




Yearly Dry Spill Counts Under Alternative Methods
Comparison of dry-spill estimation methods vs. published EA/BBC benchmarks




Methodology Notes:

Rainfall Coverage: 1km² uses the nearest grid; 9km² uses a 3×3 grid around each site (BBC).
Temporal Window: 2-day (day 0–1) vs 4-day (day 0–3) lookback.
Data Handling: Complete requires full rainfall coverage; Partial OK allows missing values when applying the 0.25mm threshold.
Highlighting: Light green marks the preferred method for each sample; Light orange marks the published benchmark.





The BBC—using a more conservative methodology—reports 6,000 dry spills in 2022. To create a comparable sample, we exclude Welsh Water and remove any United Utilities overflow site where the annual spill count exceeds 25% of the EA’s published total for that site.5 (see also Section 2.2). This adjustment removes 36% of United Utilities’ sites, broadly matching the BBC’s approach.
Our closest estimates apply the criterion of no measurable rainfall (≥0.25 mm) in the preceding four days within a 9 \(km^2\) buffer around each spill site with complete rainfall coverage (column 4). However, we prefer the version that allows for sites with missing rainfall data (column 5), since—as shown in the map below—missing data occurs almost exclusively along the coast, where excluding sites would not be meaningful.




Yearly Dry Spill Counts Under Alternative Methods
Comparison of dry-spill estimation methods vs. published EA/BBC benchmarks




Methodology Notes:

Rainfall Coverage: 1km² uses the nearest grid; 9km² uses a 3×3 grid around each site (BBC).
Temporal Window: 2-day (day 0–1) vs 4-day (day 0–3) lookback.
Data Handling: Complete requires full rainfall coverage; Partial OK allows missing values when applying the 0.25mm threshold.
Highlighting: Light green marks the preferred method for each sample; Light orange marks the published benchmark.





Going forward, we therefore report results using two definitions only: (i) no measurable rainfall (≥0.25 mm) in the preceding two days within a 9 \(km^2\) buffer, and (ii) no measurable rainfall in the preceding four days within a 9 \(km^2\) buffer.

🟠 Orange: Sites with dry spills under the 4-day window with partial data allowed

4.5 Daily Rainfall and Spill Probability
At the daily site level, spill_count is binary (0/1), so the relevant outcome for rainfall regressions is spill incidence (spill_any). We therefore estimate distributed-lag logit models for the probability of a spill day and use spill hours to describe intensity distributions across rainfall regimes.



Daily spill_count is binary in the estimation sample

4.5.1 Distributed-Lag Regressions (Logit)
To quantify how rainfall at different horizons predicts spill incidence, we estimate distributed-lag logit models where the dependent variable is a binary indicator for whether a spill occurred on a given site-day. We include contemporaneous rainfall and up to four or seven daily lags, clustering standard errors at the site level to account for serial correlation within overflow sites.

All coefficients are highly significant and positive, confirming that rainfall raises the probability of a spill. The effect is heavily front-loaded: contemporaneous rainfall has the largest coefficient (≈0.099 in log-odds), with the first lag nearly as large (≈0.092). By the second day the coefficient drops by roughly two-thirds, and it continues to decay gradually through lags 3–7. Even at lag 7, the effect remains statistically significant (≈0.009), suggesting that antecedent moisture conditions matter for at least a week—consistent with the time required for stormwater to percolate through catchments and reach the sewer network.
The 7-lag specification yields only a modestly higher pseudo-\(R^2\) (0.174 vs. 0.172), indicating that the first five days capture the bulk of the predictive content. This supports the use of short look-back windows (2–4 days) for dry-spill classification, as adopted by the EA and BBC (see ?sec-04-01-dry-spill-definitions).

4.5.1.1 Distributed-Lag Coefficient Plot









The coefficient plot makes the decay structure visually apparent: the effect of rainfall on spill probability is concentrated in the first two days, with a sharp drop-off between lag 1 and lag 2. The two specifications overlap closely for lags 0–4, while the additional lags 5–7 remain positive but small, with confidence intervals narrowing toward zero.



4.5.2 0.25 mm Threshold Models
The continuous rainfall specifications above confirm that more rain means more spills. We now ask a complementary question: does the absence of rain—defined using the 0.25 mm threshold from ?sec-04-01-dry-spill-definitions—predict lower spill probability? This directly tests the binary classification that underlies our dry-spill counts. We replace the continuous rainfall lags with dry-day indicators (\(\mathbb{1}[\text{rain} < 0.25\text{mm}]\)) at each lag.

All dry-day coefficients are large, negative, and highly significant, confirming that dry conditions substantially reduce the odds of a spill. Contemporaneous dryness has the strongest effect (≈−1.17 in log-odds in the 8-day specification), followed closely by the first lag (≈−0.99). The magnitude drops sharply at lag 2 (≈−0.36) and stabilises around −0.11 to −0.15 for lags 3–7. This mirrors the continuous-rainfall results: the first two days carry the most information, with diminishing but non-negligible effects persisting through the week.
The two-day specification—using only \(t\) and \(t{-}1\)—captures the dominant share of the dry-day signal, providing empirical support for the EA’s two-day look-back window. The four-day window used by the BBC adds further conservatism but only modest additional explanatory power.









The coefficient plot reinforces the step-function pattern: the dry-day effect is strongest at lags 0 and 1, drops abruptly at lag 2, and flattens thereafter. This suggests that the 0.25 mm threshold effectively separates “wet” from “dry” conditions for the purpose of spill classification, and that the most informative look-back is two days. The persistence of small but significant effects at longer lags is consistent with slow drainage in some catchments, but the marginal predictive gain from extending the window beyond four days is limited.


4.5.3 Spill-Hours Distribution by Rain Regime
The logit models above analyse whether a spill occurs; they say nothing about how long it lasts. We now examine the intensity of spills, measured in hours, across four rainfall regimes: dry day (≤0.25 mm), moderate rain (>0.25-10 mm), heavy rain (>10-20 mm), and very heavy rain (>20 mm).

The gradient across regimes remains steep and monotonic under this broader classification. Dry days are associated with the lowest spill incidence and shortest events, while moderate, heavy, and very heavy rainfall days show progressively higher spill probabilities and longer spill durations. The upper tail of the spill-hours distribution also thickens as rainfall intensifies, indicating that both the likelihood and duration of spills increase materially once daily rainfall rises above the dry-day threshold.









The empirical CDFs confirm that the dry-day distribution is stacked heavily at zero: the curve rises almost vertically near the origin for the Dry day <=0.25 regime, indicating that the vast majority of dry site-days record no spill at all. Wetter regimes shift the distribution rightward, with the Very heavy >20 curve notably flatter and reaching higher spill-hour values before converging to 1. The separation between regimes is clearest in the lower tail, suggesting that rainfall primarily affects whether a spill occurs rather than its duration conditional on occurrence.









The violin plots, restricted to site-days where a spill did occur, show that even conditional on spilling, wetter regimes produce longer events. The median spill duration increases with rainfall, and the upper tails of the distributions stretch progressively further. However, the overlap between regimes is substantial: short spills occur even under heavy rainfall, and long spills occasionally arise on relatively dry days. This dispersion likely reflects heterogeneity in sewer capacity, catchment size, and local drainage conditions.
Taken together, the results in this section provide strong empirical support for rainfall-based dry-spill classification. Contemporaneous and one-day-lagged rainfall are the dominant predictors of spill incidence; the 0.25 mm threshold effectively discriminates between spill-prone and non-spill conditions; and spill intensity increases monotonically across rainfall regimes. The two-day look-back window captures the bulk of the rainfall signal, while extending to four days adds conservatism with only marginal predictive gain—consistent with the methodological choices validated in Section 4.2.

title: 5  House Data Exploration
section: 5.3 House Price and Sewage Spills Descriptive Statistics
text:
5.3 House Price and Sewage Spills Descriptive Statistics

5.3.1 Cross section
I begin by examining the cross-sectional relationship between house prices and sewage spills, which can be characterised along three main dimensions: (i) house prices, (ii) spill severity (measured by the number of spills or the spill duration), and (iii) the distance to the nearest spill.
Each house sale is spatially matched to all spill sites within a 5km radius, allowing me to compute the average spill severity and the average distance to the nearest spill for different distance thresholds. This allows me to see if there are any correlations between house prices and spill severity or distance.


5.3.2 Summary Tables
I present summary statistics for the spill data aggregated over both (i) different distance thresholds and (ii) spill count quartiles within a 2km radius. I use the entire dataset, trimming the house prices to the 5th and 95th percentiles (as I don’t believe these belong to the distribution we are interested in).
There are a few patterns that stand out:

House prices increase within buffers of increasing distance from spill sites.
House prices decrease across spill count quartiles.
House prices remain positively skewed, even after trimming the 5th and 95th percentiles.
Spill counts are positively skewed (as we also saw in Section 5.2.2)


By DistanceBy Spill Count





















5.3.3 Bivariate Plots
Below I plot the relationship between house prices and spill severity (spill count and spill duration), minimum distance to spill sites, and spill severity weighted by the inverse of the distance to the nearest spill (accounting for the possibility that closer spills have a larger impact on house prices). I do this for a sample of 300,000 houses (approximately 3% of all houses in the dataset) to reduce computation time. I trim the data to the 5th and 95th percentiles of house prices (as I don’t believe these belong to the distribution we are interested in).
For each relationship, I plot a linear regression line and a local regression line (non-parametric) to show potential linear and non-linear relationships. I present these relationships using data aggregated over all years as well as data aggregated over the trailing 12-month period.

5.3.3.1 Spill Count
There’s a negative cross-sectional linear relationship between spill count and house price. The negative correlation is stronger both for (i) the trailing 12 months compared to the full time period and (ii) smaller distance thresholds.
For distance thresholds below 2km, the non-parametric fit shows a U-shaped pattern, likely due to a few properties with both high spill counts and high prices. However, as the distance threshold increases, the curvature of the U-shape flattens. I have to look further into this, but this may be driven by water company-owned properties, which could have higher prices and be exposed to more spills since they located closer to the spill sites (see also Section 5.3.3.3).

LinearAll Years - Linear and LOESS12 Months - Linear and LOESS






































5.3.3.2 Spill Duration
The relationship between spill duration and house price is comparable to the relationship between spill count and house price. The confidence intervals are larger, suggesting greater statistical uncertainty. It seems plausible that there is greater variation in spill duration since duration records are influenced by extreme events or measurement errors that affect how long a spill lasts, whereas the 12/24 count method aggregates events more discretely.1

LinearAll Years - Linear and LOESS12 Months - Linear and LOESS






































5.3.3.3 Distance
There’s a negative cross-sectional linear relationship between distance to the nearest spill site (within thresholds) and house price for tresholds below 1km, and slight positive relationship for larger thresholds. There are no apparent differences between the trailing 12 months and the full time period.
For all thresholds, the non-parametric fit shows a U-shaped pattern, which is consinstent with the story of water company-owned properties, which could have higher prices and be exposed to more spills since they located closer to the spill sites (see also Section 5.3.3.1).

LinearAll Years - Linear and LOESS12 Months - Linear and LOESS






































5.3.3.4 Inverse Spill Count - Distance
There’s a negative cross-sectional linear relationship between inverse spill count and house price. The negative correlation is stronger both for (i) the trailing 12 months compared to the full time period and (ii) the larger distance thresholds. Additionally, the non-parametric fit shows a U-shaped pattern. Both of these observations are consistent with the story of water company-owned properties, which could have higher prices and be exposed to more spills since they located closer to the spill sites (see also Section 5.3.3.1 and Section 5.3.3.3).

LinearAll Years - Linear and LOESS12 Months - Linear and LOESS

This enables cross-sectional analyses by distance threshold and spill severity.

title: 7  Event Study
text:
7.1 Setting
This section is currently being revised as I read and process the recent DiD and event study literature. Some statements may be unclear or incorrect, which I will try to refine them as I go.
We can conceptualise the empirical setting as an event study, where treatment occurs when a location experiences a sewage spill above a defined threshold, and house prices serve as the outcome variable.
Given the potential problems with the vanilla TWFE presented in the recent literature, I decide to use the Chaisemartin & d’Haultefoeuille (2024) estimator using the package DIDmultiplegtDYN. This estimator allows for a wide range of settings, including non-absorbing treatment, staggered treatment, continuous treatment, and many other settings that I still need to understand.

title: 7  Event Study
text:
Absorbing vs. non-absorbing treatment: Treatment status can be defined in two ways. In a non-absorbing framework, locations may alternate between treated and control states, depending on spill severity in each period. For example, a location may experience a large spill in one quarter and no spill in the next quarter. Alternatively, in an absorbing framework, a location is considered treated from the first period it exceeds the threshold and remains treated thereafter.
Treatment is staggered: Spill events, and therefore treatment, occur at different times across different locations.
Treatment measurement: Spill severity is measured by either the number of incidents or by total duration in a given period \(t\) (e.g., monthly or quarterly). While these measures are continuous, we can construct a binary treatment indicator by classifying a house as treated if it lies within radius X of a spill site whose severity exceeds a predetermined threshold.
Fixed effects: To (partially) address confounding, we include both time and location fixed effects. This ensures that identification relies on within-location variation over time, controlling for any time-invariant differences across locations and common shocks across periods.

title: 7  Event Study
section: 7.2 House Sales: Site-Level Event Study Regressions Within Spill Buffers
text:
7.2 House Sales: Site-Level Event Study Regressions Within Spill Buffers
The following figures present the dynamic effects of sewage spills on house prices within an X-metre radius around spill sites. For each spill site and quarter, I aggregate spill statistics and average house prices within the corresponding X-metre buffer.1 The estimated regression coefficients capture the within-buffer change in average house prices over time, relative to untreated periods, conditional on the buffer experiencing a high-severity spill in a given quarter. That is, they reflect how prices evolve in a given spill site’s buffer following the occurrence of a high-severity spill within that buffer.
The regressions below are estimated using the Chaisemartin & d’Haultefoeuille (2024) estimator using the package DIDmultiplegtDYN. This estimator allows for a wide range of settings, including non-absorbing treatment, staggered treatment, continuous treatment, and many other settings that I still need to understand. In the event study regressions, the effects are estimated for:

Same switchers only. This ensures that event-study effects are estimated only using switchers for whom all the event-study effects can be estimated. This avoids the problem that if treatment effects are heterogeneous across spill sites where treatment switches, changes in the estimated effects across different time horizons might reflect changes in the composition of the sample rather than actual dynamic treatment effects.
Normalised. This scales the estimates to represent effects per unit of treatment rather than total effects. For spill sites, this means showing the impact per unit of treatment intensity, making estimates comparable across time periods and sites.

title: 7  Event Study
section: 7.3 House Sales: House-Level Event Study Regressions Within Admin Boundaries
text:
7.3 House Sales: House-Level Event Study Regressions Within Admin Boundaries
The advantages of using house-level data relative to site-level data in the event study regressions are:

House-level controls: Working at the house-level allows us to include property attributes (e.g. newly built, property type etc.) as controls. This ensures that any post-spill changes in the composition of sales do not confound price effects.
No double-counting from overlapping buffers: Each property appears only once, even if it falls within 500 m of multiple outfalls. This avoids mechanical correlation among treated observations, which could inflate standard errors.
Larger control group: Properties outside of the X-metre buffer remain in the sample. We can therefore use different location fixed effects (e.g., LSOA, MSOA, Admin Ward, Admin County, County, Region, Country, NHS HA) to account for any pre-existing differences in house prices across the study area.

The figures below show the dynamic effects of sewage spills on house prices across different administrative boundaries. Using geocoded transaction data, I calculate the total volume of sewage spills within an Xm radius of each property for every calendar quarter. A property is classified as treated in a given quarter if the spill volume exceeds a specified threshold. This approach allows treatment timing to vary across properties, depending on the occurrence and intensity of nearby spills.
The regressions estimate changes in house prices over time within the same location, comparing treated and untreated periods. Regressions are estimated both without controls (NC) and with controls (C):

Old/New: A binary variable indicating whether the property was a newly built property that had not been previosly sold.
Property Type: A categorical variable indicating the type of property (detached, semi-detached, terraced, flats/maisonettes, other)
Duration: A binary variable indicating the tenure of the property (freehold, leasehold)

Regressions are estimated using the event-study estimator developed by Sun and Abraham (2021).

title: 8  Hedonics Regressions
text: 8.1 House-Level Hedonic Regressions

title: 8  Hedonics Regressions
text:
8.1.1 Setting
We employ hedonic regression analysis to quantify the monetary impact of sewage spills on residential property values. This approach statistically isolates the effect of varying sewage spill volumes within a defined proximity to homes, while controlling for other property and locational characteristics. The hedonic methodology enables us to estimate the implicit price adjustment—either premium or discount—that buyers apply when sewage spills are present.
The results below examine hedonic regressions across three spatial radii (250m, 500m, and 1000m) around each property. For each transaction, I calculate the total volume of sewage spills within the specified radius for every calendar quarter. Properties located beyond the radius boundary are assigned a spill statistic of zero. I also calculate the inverse of the minimum distance to the nearest sewage spill site, which serves as a spatial control variable, allowing us to account for the proximity effect of sewage spills on property values.
The spill exposure variables are constructed using two temporal aggregation methods:

Lagged statistic: Total sewage spill volume in the quarter immediately preceding the property transaction
Rolling statistic: Cumulative sewage spill volume over the four quarters preceding the property transaction

Regressions are estimated both without controls (NC) and with controls (C):

Old/New: A binary variable indicating whether the property was a newly built property that had not been previosly sold.
Property Type: A categorical variable indicating the type of property (detached, semi-detached, terraced, flats/maisonettes, other)
Duration: A binary variable indicating the tenure of the property (freehold, leasehold)

title: 9  Pipeline Overview
text:
9.1 Pipeline Organisation
The current data cleaning pipeline consists of 20 R scripts that process sewage discharge data, house price data, and spatial relationships to create datasets for analysis.
The pipeline is structured across 6 layers:

title: 9  Pipeline Overview
text:
Data Ingestion Layer (01_data_ingestion/): This layer contains scripts that retrieve or extract unprocessed data from external sources—such as downloading from APIs or unpacking zip archives. The scripts here do not clean or transform data; they only collect and save it locally.
Data Cleaning Layer (02_data_cleaning/): This layer includes scripts that clean, reformat, and validate raw data files. These scripts ensure consistency across files by fixing column names, data types, missing values, and file formats. They produce uniform, structured outputs usable downstream.
Data Enrichment Layer (03_data_enrichment/): This layer holds scripts that enhance cleaned data with additional structure—by generating lookup tables, calculating summaries, or tagging records with geographic or temporal metadata. These scripts prepare the data for matching and analysis by adding context.
Feature Engineering Layer (04_feature_engineering/): This layer includes scripts that generate analysis-ready variables. This involves creating spatial matches, calculating distances, defining treatment indicators, and building other derived features required for regressions or causal analysis.
Integration Layer (05_data_integration/): This layer contains scripts that merge datasets from different sources or time periods. These scripts align records across formats, join data on shared identifiers, and harmonise inconsistencies to produce a single dataset structure.
Analysis Datasets Layer (06_analysis_datasets/): This layer includes scripts that build the final analysis datasets. These scripts define the unit of observation (e.g. house-level, site-level), assign exposure variables, and output clean panels or cross-sections used directly in empirical estimation.

title: 9  Pipeline Overview
text:
9.2 Flowchart
The flowchart below illustrates the data cleaning pipeline, showing how data flows through each layer and the dependencies between scripts.

title: 9  Pipeline Overview
text:
9.3 Scripts Overview

9.3.1 Raw Data Ingestion Layer
EDM Data Standardisation (2021-2023)

script_name: edm_individ_data_standardisation_2021-2023.R
purpose: Standardises raw Event Duration Monitoring (EDM) data files from UK water companies for 2021-2023 by extracting, renaming, and organising files from zip archives.
inputs: ["data/raw/edm_data/EDM*.zip"]
outputs: ["data/raw/edm_data/YYYY_company_name_edm.*"]

API Data Fetching (2024+)

script_name: fetch_edm_api_data_2024_onwards.R
purpose: Downloads and processes EDM data from ArcGIS API endpoints for 2024 onwards using pagination and saves as compressed JSON files.
inputs: ["scripts/config/api_config.R"]
outputs:

"data/raw/edm_data/raw_api_responses/company_name/*.json.gz"
"output/log/edm_api_download_log.csv"




9.3.2 Data Cleaning & Standardisation Layer
Raw Data Conversion

script_name: convert_individ_raw_data_to_rdata_2021-2023.R
purpose: Converts and cleans individual raw EDM data files from CSV/Excel formats to standardised Parquet format for years 2021-2023.
inputs: ["data/raw/edm_data/YYYY_company_name_edm.*"]
outputs:

"data/processed/edm_data_2021_2023/*.parquet"
"data/processed/edm_data_2021_2023/combined_edm_data_*.parquet"


Individual EDM Data Combination

script_name: combine_individ_edm_data_2021-2023.R
purpose: Combines and cleans individual water company Parquet files from 2021-2023 into a single consolidated dataset with standardised datetime parsing.
inputs: ["data/processed/edm_data_2021_2023/*.parquet"]
outputs: ["data/processed/combined_edm_data.parquet"]

API Data Processing

script_name: process_edm_api_json_to_parquet_2024_onwards.R
purpose: Processes raw API JSON data into standardised Parquet files, extracts features data, and handles incremental updates for 2024 onwards.
inputs: ["data/raw/edm_data/raw_api_responses/company_name/*.json.gz"]
outputs: ["data/processed/edm_api_data/*.parquet"]

API Data Combination

script_name: combine_api_edm_data_2024_onwards.R
purpose: Combines and cleans individual water company API Parquet files from 2024 onwards into a single consolidated dataset.
inputs: ["data/processed/edm_api_data/*.parquet"]
outputs: ["data/processed/edm_api_data/combined_api_data.parquet"]

Annual Return Data Processing

script_name: combine_annual_return_data.R
purpose: Cleans and combines annual sewage spill return data from UK water companies for 2021-2024, standardising column names and formats.
inputs: ["data/raw/edm_data/YYYY_annual_return_edm.xlsx"]
outputs: ["data/processed/annual_return_edm.parquet"]

Consented Discharges Database Cleaning

script_name: clean_consented_discharges_database.R
purpose: Cleans the Environment Agency’s consented discharges database, standardising water company names and column mappings.
inputs: ["data/raw/ea_consents/consents_all.csv"]
outputs:

"data/processed/consent_discharges_db.RData"
"data/processed/consent_discharges_db.csv"


House Price Data Cleaning

script_name: clean_lr_house_price_data.R
purpose: Cleans and combines HM Land Registry UK property sales data for 2021-2023, enriching with postcode coordinates using PostcodesioR API.
inputs: ["data/raw/lr_house_price/pp-YYYY.csv"]
outputs: ["data/processed/house_price.parquet"]



9.3.3 Data Enrichment & Aggregation Layer
Annual Return Lookup Creation

script_name: create_annual_return_lookup.R
purpose: Builds a cross-year lookup table linking the same spill sites across annual EDM returns (2021-2024) using deterministic and probabilistic matching.
inputs: ["data/processed/annual_return_edm.parquet"]
outputs:

"data/processed/annual_return_lookup.parquet"
"data/processed/annual_return_lookup_edges.parquet"
"data/processed/annual_return_lookup.xlsx"


Spill Statistics Aggregation

script_name: aggregate_spill_stats.R
purpose: Aggregates individual sewage overflow spills into monthly, quarterly, and yearly statistics using the 12/24 hour counting method.
inputs: ["data/processed/matched_events_annual_data/matched_events_annual_data.parquet"]
outputs:

"data/processed/agg_spill_stats/agg_spill_yr.parquet"
"data/processed/agg_spill_stats/agg_spill_mo.parquet"
"data/processed/agg_spill_stats/agg_spill_qtr.parquet"


Unique Spill Sites Creation

script_name: create_unique_spill_sites.R
purpose: Creates unique spill sites dataset by cleaning location data and parsing British National Grid coordinates from merged EDM data.
inputs: ["data/processed/matched_events_annual_data/matched_events_annual_data.parquet"]
outputs:

"data/processed/unique_spill_sites.parquet"
"data/processed/unique_spill_sites.xlsx"




9.3.4 Feature Engineering & Spatial Processing Layer
Spatial Matching

script_name: 10km_site_house_sale_match.R
purpose: Performs spatial matching between house sales and spill sites within 10km radius using spatial joins and distance calculations.
inputs:

"data/processed/house_price.parquet"
"data/processed/unique_spill_sites.parquet"

outputs: ["data/processed/spill_house_lookup.parquet"]

Spill Statistics Computing

script_name: compute_spill_stats.R
purpose: Calculates site-level spill statistics including dry weather spill metrics, cross-site thresholds, and treatment indicators for monthly and quarterly periods. Computes period-specific, yearly, and all-time thresholds for both total spills and dry spills with binary indicator creation.
inputs:

"data/processed/agg_spill_stats/agg_spill_dry_mo.parquet"
"data/processed/agg_spill_stats/agg_spill_dry_qtr.parquet"

outputs:

"data/processed/agg_spill_stats/agg_spill_stats_mo.parquet"
"data/processed/agg_spill_stats/agg_spill_stats_qtr.parquet"




9.3.5 Integration Layer
Historical and API Data Integration

script_name: combine_2021-2023_and_api_edm_data.R
purpose: Harmonises and combines the processed 2021-2023 EDM data with API-sourced 2024+ data into a single consolidated dataset.
inputs:

"data/processed/combined_edm_data.parquet"
"data/processed/edm_api_data/combined_api_data.parquet"

outputs: ["data/processed/combined_edm_data.parquet"]

Individual and Annual Data Merging

script_name: merge_individ_annual_location.R
purpose: Merges location data from annual sewage overflow datasets with individual spill data using windfall, many-to-many, and fuzzy matching methods.
inputs:

"data/processed/combined_edm_data.parquet"
"data/processed/annual_return_edm.parquet"
"data/processed/annual_return_lookup.parquet"

outputs:

"data/processed/matched_events_annual_data/matched_events_annual_data.parquet"
"data/processed/matched_events_annual_data/events_unmatched.parquet"
"data/processed/matched_events_annual_data/annual_unmatched.parquet"
"data/processed/matched_events_annual_data/site_metadata.parquet"




9.3.6 Master Dataset Assembly Layer
Cross-Sectional Database Creation (Sales)

script_name: cross_section_sales.R
purpose: Creates cross-sectional databases aggregating spill data: one for all years and one for 12 months prior to house sales, partitioned by radius thresholds.
inputs:

"data/processed/house_price.parquet"
"data/processed/spill_house_lookup.parquet"
"data/processed/agg_spill_stats/agg_spill_mo.parquet"

outputs:

"data/processed/cross_section/sales/all_years/"
"data/processed/cross_section/sales/prior_12mo/"


Cross-Sectional Database Creation (Rentals)

script_name: cross_section_rental.R
purpose: Creates cross-sectional rental datasets: all years and 12 months prior to rented_est, partitioned by radius thresholds.
inputs:

"data/processed/zoopla/zoopla_rentals.parquet"
"data/processed/zoopla/spill_rental_lookup.parquet"
"data/processed/agg_spill_stats/agg_spill_mo.parquet"

outputs:

"data/processed/cross_section/rentals/all_years/"
"data/processed/cross_section/rentals/prior_12mo/"


Site-Level Panel Data (Sales)

script_name: site_panel_sales.R
purpose: Creates site-level panel data by aggregating house price statistics (median, mean, weighted) for each spill site across multiple distance radii and time periods.
inputs:

"data/processed/house_price.parquet"
"data/processed/spill_house_lookup.parquet"

outputs: ["data/processed/dat_panel_site/sales/"]

Site-Level Panel Data (Rentals)

script_name: site_panel_rental.R
purpose: Builds site-level panel datasets of rental prices (median, mean, weighted) around each spill site for specified distance radii and periods.
inputs:

"data/processed/zoopla/zoopla_rentals.parquet"
"data/processed/zoopla/spill_rental_lookup.parquet"

outputs: ["data/processed/dat_panel_site/rentals/"]

House Panel Within Radius

script_name: house_panel_within_radius.R
purpose: Creates house sale-level panel data containing only houses within specified radius thresholds of spill sites, with monthly and quarterly aggregation.
inputs:

"data/processed/house_price.parquet"
"data/processed/spill_house_lookup.parquet"

outputs: ["data/processed/within_radius_panel/sales/"]

Rental Panel Within Radius

script_name: rental_panel_within_radius.R
purpose: Builds rental listing-level panel data containing only listings within specified radii of spill sites, with monthly and quarterly aggregation.
inputs:

"data/processed/zoopla/zoopla_rentals.parquet"
"data/processed/zoopla/spill_rental_lookup.parquet"

outputs: ["data/processed/within_radius_panel/rentals/"]

General House Panel

script_name: sale_panel_exp.R
purpose: Creates panel datasets linking house sale transactions to nearby sewage spill sites across different radii. The datasets can be joined with the house_price data (for house price data), and spill statistics data.
inputs:

"data/processed/house_price.parquet"
"data/processed/spill_house_lookup.parquet"
"data/processed/agg_spill_stats/"

outputs: ["data/processed/general_panel/sales/"]

General Rental Panel

script_name: rental_panel_exp.R
purpose: Creates panel datasets linking rental transactions to nearby sewage spill sites across different radii. The datasets can be joined with the zoopla_rentals data, and spill statistics data.
inputs:

"data/processed/zoopla/zoopla_rentals.parquet"
"data/processed/zoopla/spill_rental_lookup.parquet"
"data/processed/agg_spill_stats/"

outputs: ["data/processed/general_panel/rentals/"]

title: 10  Ingestion Layer
text:
10.1 Overview of Ingestion Layer
The ingestion layer is responsible for acquiring raw data and preparing it for downstream use. It handles data collection, retrieval, and basic organisation, but performs no cleaning or transformation. Its purpose is to ensure that all inputs to the pipeline are systematically acquired and stored in a standardised structure.
Within this project, the ingestion layer is only required for the two sources of data:

title: 10  Ingestion Layer
text:
EDM 2021–2023: Individual spill data obtained via a Freedom of Information request, received in a variety of file formats from water companies.
EDM 2024-present: Individual spill data obtained from ArcGIS API endpoints provided by Water UK.1

title: 10  Ingestion Layer
text:
10.2 2021-2023 EDM Data Ingestion
The 2021–2023 EDM data was obtained via FOI requests from multiple water companies, each using different formats and naming conventions. The script edm_individ_data_standardisation_2021-2023.R standardises the raw files at the ingestion stage to ensure consistent structure and naming.
It extracts files from .zip archives, identifies those with .csv, .xlsx, or .xlsb extensions, and renames them using the format YYYY_CompanyName_edm.ext, based on metadata inferred from the original filenames. The script performs only file system operations and does not load or modify the data. All standardised files are moved to a single directory for downstream processing.

title: 10  Ingestion Layer
text:
10.3 2024-Present EDM Data Ingestion
Individual EDM data for 2024 onwards is available via ArcGIS API endpoints provided by Water UK. This data is ingested through a two-script pipeline designed for incremental, timestamped updates as new spill records are released.

Automated Retrieval: Script fetch_edm_api_data_2024_onwards.R queries API endpoints for each water company, as defined in an external configuration file. It handles pagination, consolidates results into a single JSON object, and saves a timestamped, compressed file (e.g., 250405_1030_thames_water.json.gz). Each retrieval is logged in a persistent CSV.
Incremental Processing Script process_edm_api_json_to_parquet_2024_onwards.R identifies unprocessed .json.gz files and extracts the relevant features array from each.
Standardisation: Extracted records are cleaned by standardising column names to snake_case and tagging each entry with a water_company identifier.
Deduplication and Update: New records are appquarto publish gh-pagesended to the company’s master Parquet file. The combined dataset is de-duplicated to ensure all entries are unique.
Archiving: After processing, source .json.gz files are moved to a processed subdirectory to prevent reprocessing.

title: 10  Ingestion Layer
text: As of July 2025, the API data for 2024 is excluded from the analysis due to major gaps—fewer than 2,000 spill records are available, compared to an expected 450,000. The ingestion infrastructure is ready to incorporate this data once it becomes complete.↩︎
